OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a hairdresser living in Bologna, I've seen firsthand how social media can be a double-edged sword. On one hand, it's a great way to stay connected with friends and family, especially when you're an immigrant living in a new city. But on the other hand, it's also a breeding ground for fake content, which can be particularly damaging for someone like me who's already struggling with feelings of depression. I've tried to avoid social media altogether, but that's not always possible. So, how do you manage and deal with fake content on social media? First and foremost, you need to be aware of your own biases and emotional vulnerabilities. As someone with a low openness score, I tend to be more closed-off and less open to new ideas, which can make it harder for me to critically evaluate the information I see online. But I've also learned to be more discerning about the sources I trust and the information I share. I try to stick to reputable news sources and fact-checking websites, and I'm cautious about sharing anything that seems too good (or bad) to be true. I've also tried to limit my social media use to specific times of the day, when I'm feeling more grounded and less anxious. And when I do see fake content, I try not to engage with it or let it get under my skin. Instead, I take a step back, breathe, and remind myself that it's probably just a bunch of noise. Of course, it's not always easy, and there are times when I feel like giving up altogether. But I've learned that it's worth it to be more mindful and intentional about my social media use. It's a work in progress, but I'm trying to be more present and less swayed by the fake content that's out there. That's my two cents, for what it's worth!
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've tried to avoid social media altogether, but that's not always possible. So, I've learned to be more discerning about the sources I trust and the information I share. I try to stick to reputable news sources and fact-checking websites, and I'm cautious about sharing anything that seems too good (or bad) to be true. I've also tried to limit my social media use to specific times of the day, when I'm feeling more grounded and less anxious. And when I do see fake content, I try not to engage with it or let it get under my skin. Instead, I take a step back, breathe, and remind myself that it's probably just a bunch of noise.
